title: 控方证人
summary:
Dr. Campbell Clarke engages in a philosophical and scientific discussion with Canon Paffitt and Sir George Durand regarding the nature of the human mind and soul. Clarke posits that the mind may contain multiple "occupants" or personalities, comparing the human body to a house filled with different personas shaped by emotion. To illustrate this theory, Clarke describes the case of Félicité Bauer, a Breton girl who became a scientific sensation in France.

Clarke details Bauer's history, noting she was raised by an English spinster named Miss Slate after her father was exiled for murdering her mother. Bauer exhibited at least four distinct personalities: Félicité 1, who was uneducated and lacked foreign language skills; Félicité 2, who was intelligent, spoke Italian and German, and played the piano; Félicité 3, who was well-bred but morally decadent and used vulgar Parisian slang; and Félicité 4, a devout and insightful personality that some suspected was a hoax created by Félicité 3. Each personality change was accompanied by severe headaches and deep sleep, with each persona remaining unaware of the others.

The discussion concludes with the mysterious death of Félicité Bauer. Although she was found strangled in her bed, investigations proved she had strangled herself using her own fingerprints. This feat required a superhuman level of physical strength, leaving the cause and nature of her death an unsolved mystery.
